replies.content:
My response is general, and I have no idea whether it applies to your specific question. I do interpret my dreams from time to time. There are periods where dreams seem significant. Or once in a blue moon, one whacks me hard upside the head. Interpretation for me is never immediate. First, I really only attempt to interpret those that have emotional impact. I write them down promptly in a journal. I also journal what is going on in life (and/or weighing on my mind) when the dream occurred. I might make it a focus of mediation; and consider it a puzzle to work on over the long haul. One of my most significant dreams took 30 years to interpret(i.e. I had a devastating nightmare at age 3 or 4; it's intolerable meaning only declared itself to me as I sat beside my dying father when I was 33). Sometimes dreams occur in a sequence, and a meaning will declare itself if you notice the sequence. People often speak of dreams recurring, which I've certainly had, but more commonly, they rhyme. Sometimes the emotion you experience during the dream is key to its meaning, and make sure you journal that, and see if you recognize that emotion in waking life, and what circumstances are associated. Pay attention to what is bothering you, and/or to what SHOULD BE bothering you; meditate and journal on this. Recurring/rhyming/emotion-laden/connected dreams have been significant for me, and I'm sure this is generally true. I consider a dream to be interpreted when its meaning presents itself as a sudden insight.

This is a very off-the cuff reply, I'm sure it could be more clear and comprehensive. I hope it helps a bit.

--Lunar Apprentice

comments.title: Concerning Theurgy in the Iamblichus Neoplatonic tradition
comments.content:
Hello JMG,
I am reading Theurgy and the Soul by Shaw. Iamblichus stressed the need for the ritual of Theurgy in order to make a connection with the gods in the act of Demiurgy, but I have yet to read directly what Iamblichus did that he called "theurgy". Is the act of Theurgy somewhat similar to the rituals such as the SOP? If not, would you mind explaining what Theurgy actually is?
Many thanks!

replies.title: Re: Concerning Theurgy in the Iamblichus Neoplatonic tradition
replies.content: We have only a very sketchy idea about how Iamblichean theurgy was practiced, since none of the rituals have survived. It may have had a fair amount in common with the rituals of the Graeco-Egyptian magical papyri, but that's only a guess. Certainly it involved rituals that brought the mage into contact with beneficent spirits and the gods, and so it probably had a certain amount in common with modern ceremonial magic...but again, we just don't know.

replies.title: Re: Concerning Theurgy in the Iamblichus Neoplatonic tradition
replies.content: For what it's worth, the Chaldean Oracles have the earliest known mention of the word "theurgy", and were considered fairly authoritative by many of the neo-Platonists. This is only somewhat helpful, since we only have fragments of the Oracles, and they leave out key details like the actual text of the incantations you're supposed to say. But it's probably the best place to start if you want to investigate further.

replies.title: Re: Concerning Theurgy in the Iamblichus Neoplatonic tradition
replies.content:
For whatever it's worth, I came up with a heavily syncretic folk practice from the part of the world inhabited by characters like Tommaso Campanella (i.e. we were never told why there was a four-foot tall statue of Athena in my aunt's living room that dwarfed all the Christian artefacts...) I had praxis and myths, and had seen some both benefic and malefic stuff over the years, but had absolutely no idea of they "why" of any of it...until I found JMG's Galabes blog, which led by me to Ficino and that era and finally back to Proclus and Iamblichus (many thanks, JMG). In short, Neoplatonism helped me understand that what my family had all along was a kind of very folk theurgy, which in practice seems to jibe with things similar to Golden Dawn practice (FTR, I have no formal experience with the latter, but seems so from the little I have read.)

All of which is to say: while the exact practices of the old Neoplatonists seem to be lost, it seems almost certain that the operational effects of such practices is a type of theurgy, and I find that framework now essential to contextualizing the practice.

Axé,
Fra' Lupo

replies.title: Re: Concerning Theurgy in the Iamblichus Neoplatonic tradition
replies.content:
Although this is some weeks ago and unlikely that folks might revisit this thread, ya never know: I stumbled across a pretty interesting paper that evaluates the continuity between "modern" practices such as the Golden Dawn and premodern practices stretching back through Ficino to Iamblichus...RE-EVALUATING THE NOVELTY OF THE GOLDEN DAWN'S MAGIC, by Christopher A. comments.title: God or Not?
comments.content:
I have a question that might (or might not) be a little odd. Years ago, when I was about 15, maybe 16, I went on vacation to Michigan with my parents. While we were walking around a shopping mall there, I entered a department store and found a Ghanaian wood statue of a tall man. It was on clearance for like $3.50. I felt a strong compulsion to buy it. It quickly became one of my most prized possessions, and I still have it. I also quickly felt like there was a presence in the statue, a living presence that has watched over me since I acquired the piece. As the scales have fallen from my eyes as of late, I have begun to suspect that perhaps this statue has a divine spirit in it. Over the years, I have even felt a desire to make offerings to the statue, though I have yet to do so.

1. Might I have acquired some kind of actual idol, with a divine spirit in it?

2. If so, I'd like to make those offerings to it, though I don't know its name. Would simply offering some incense and prayers be fine?

3. How might I divine the name of this spirit, assuming there is one resident in it?

replies.title: Re: God or Not?
replies.content:
1) Yes.

2) That's a classic way of handling it.

3) When you pray to it, ask it to tell you the name it would like you to call it. See what kind of response you get.

So I've just started getting into magical practice in the Golden Dawn/Hermetic Cabala tradition - starting out with your book Paths of Wisdom, of course. 4. After practicing the First Stage meditation a few times, I automatically found myself visualising a ray of light shining upon me similar to what you're supposed to visualise for the LRP, and I felt a warm, tingling sensation focused around my solar plexus and a weird magnetic feeling on my forehead. Just tonight after meditation, I was able to briefly intensify the magnetic feeling around my forehead at will, after which my solar plexus area started to feel very hot and almost as if it was burning (although it didn't hurt). Would you say this is a sign that I'm skipping ahead too quickly and overcharging myself with energy or is this normal?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Yes, though it has more effect if you can be a little louder. You don't have to yell, however.

2) Not necessarily. It's very common for the apparent effect to decline over time as you get used to it. It's not actually having less of an effect -- it's just more familiar and so less striking.

3) It was probably just the fact that it was your first magical practice, and your subtle bodies weren't prepared for it.

4) Such things are perfectly normal. Most students get some odd energy movements early on.

replies.title: (no subject)

OK, there has been some discussion of this in the past: can I ask for a definitive ruling?
With the pagan form of the LBRP for which you published a template a few years ago, and which includes only the banishing mode, is it ever correct to use the summoning mode as well, and if so in what context?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The Lesser Summoning Ritual of the Pentagram, in the specific form I gave, is useful for opening magical space. In the Druidical Order of the Golden Dawn, which uses rituals of that form, we begin any serious ritual with the LSRP and end it with the LBRP. That's not standard practice in the Hermetic GD, and orders descended from it -- they banish on both ends -- but then they're tracing the pentagram differently. (They have good reason for tracing it their way, we have good reason for tracing it ours.)

Also, in the early phases of training, it's helpful to alternate practicing the LSRP and the LBRP so you can begin sensitizing yourself to the movement of magical energies.

comments.title: The Violet Flame
comments.content:
I'm thinking of taking up the Violet Flame meditation, with its decrees. After encountering mention of it online a couple of years ago, I considered but did not put it into practice; however I was reminded of it when I looked up the website of the Saint Germain Foundation, which you mentioned recently in the answers thread to a post on this forum. I've reviewed what's involved and I think I'm ready to go with it.

I'm wondering about compatibility issues. I practice HOGD rituals adapted for use with Hellenic deities. The imagery used in Violet Flame decrees tends to be Christian. In particular, one is advised to request protection from the archangel Michael. I like the image of the archangel Michael and could probably go with it, but might this not clash with my Hellenic Golden Dawn ritual practice? Will it be best if I keep the practices separate and distinct? Supposing instead I asked for protection from some of the Olympians - what might be likely to happen in that case?

One reason I am drawn to this practice is the claim made for it that sounds much like something you've said about discursive meditation: it helps resolve old karma, and tends eventually toward recollection of past lives. It's mainly the former that interests me.

I've found a number of decrees in verse form by Elizabeth Clare Prophet, and am thinking of using or adapting some of them. But apparently she ran the Summit Lighthouse group back in the day, and you've warned us against that as a cultish organization. Would I run the risk of becoming entangled in their egregor by using her materials, or was she active before that developed?

I've listened to some videos of her leading chants, and I didn't like her voice, nor the rhythm she gave to the chants; to my mind the Hare Krishna mantra is the gold standard of chants, as it can be - and often is - made into a beautiful song, is short and easy to remember (but not *too* short, like "Om") and is something one can put into practice right away. But the Violet Flame sounds more like it will really meet my needs.

replies.title: Re: The Violet Flame
replies.content: That's an interesting question; I don't know of anyone who's tried to combine the Ascended Masters work with Hellenic polytheism. One thing you might consider is asking Apollo to fill the role that St. Michael fills in the standard version -- they have very similar energies. As for Prophet, I don't recommend using any of her work. You'd be better off finding other decrees, or simply writing your own -- that's always an option.

replies.title: Re: The Violet Flame
replies.content: Thank you! I am forewarned against using the writings of E.C.P. I will seek other models for my decrees on the Saint Germain website. And I will try your suggestion about Apollo; I had considered appealing to him for that purpose.

We contend that all of the credible phenomena which Johansen described may be explained as being the observable consequences of a localized bubble of spacetime curvature. Many of his most incomprehensible statements (involving the geometry of the architecture, and variability of the location of the horizon) can therefore be said to have a unified underlying cause.
We propose a simplified example of such a geometry, and show using numerical computation that Johansen’s descriptions were, for the most part, not simply the ravings of a lunatic. We calculate the type of matter which would be required to generate such exotic spacetime curvature. To temper our findings, we concluded our study by asking whether Johansen’s spacetime bubble could be generated with any physically feasible matter. Sax Rohmer wrote a number of very good occult detective stories. Among my favorites are
"The Brood of the Witch Queen" (1918)
"Tales of Secret Egypt" (1918)--short stories
"The Dream Detective" (1920)
"Grey Face" (1924)

Maybe this is just new to me and not everyone else, but I am struck by just how 'magical' the field of economics is - it seems a lot of economic boom and bust cycles (easily mappable onto the Wheel of the Year as perhaps unbalanced forms of it) are as much psychologically driven as they are driven by physical limitations. Parssons looks at the history of Weimar inflation along with the post-War American inflation in the 60s, and a huge component in those inflations were decisions made based on ideology rather than observations of how I imagined that economics is supposed to work (a study of limited resources and limitations in general).

The scary thing is that it is widely assumed to be a rational science! But the very act of attributing value to units of a physical substance (money) is profoundly psychological, thus firmly in the realm of the magical. The ability of economic decisions, based on these unspoken psychological and intellectual causes, to influence the well being of people and the state of the natural world is profound.

(The book, by the way, is great. It discusses, among other things, how inflation has a seductively wonderful effect at first, as the economy and everyone in it prospers, but through time rewards mainly the non-productive classes - especially in today's terms the PMC - and causes a divide between those who can partake in the boom and those who increasingly cannot - think those left behind in housing bubbles and speculative bubbles like Bitcoin - and eventually results in periods where basically everyone loses. comments.title: The Accolade by Edmund Leighton
comments.content: I was wondering if there was particular meaning in the symbolism of the black bird with the crescent moon  that's featured in this painting. I haven't found it specifically but it looks like it could have some relation to alchemy, and was wondering if anyone has thoughts about it. Sorry couldn't figure out how to post a link to the image. Thanks always.

replies.title: Re: The Accolade by Edmund Leighton
replies.content:
Well, let's take a look.
[IMAGE:0]
The black bird is a standard heraldic eagle; his coat of arms doesn't quite follow the rules of heraldry, in that you're not supposed to put black on red. (the rule is "color on metal and metal on color;" the metals are or and argent, shown as white and yellow in most contexts, and everything else is a color.) The crescent is a mark of cadency, which is how you distinguish your arms if your dad's still alive. The man being knighted is the second son -- that's what the crescent indicates.

So yes, it has a symbolic meaning, but no, it's not alchemical. The language of heraldry is quite complex and can often tell you quite a bit about the person who bears any particular coat of arms.

replies.title: Re: The Accolade by Edmund Leighton
replies.content: If only there were a thin white or yellow border between the eagle and the red field - maybe yellow, as I think the crescent is supposed to be gold (although it looks more tawny-brown to me). Then the blazon could be something along the lines of gules, an eagle displayed sable fimbriated Or, on the eagle a crescent Or.

replies.title: Re: The Accolade by Edmund Leighton
replies.content: Exactly. The painter wasn't exactly careful about such details, though -- notice the style of the helmet. As far as I know, nobody wore an open-faced sallet like that until well after plain mail had been replaced by at least partial plate armor.

comments.title: Adam Curtis series
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

This isn't really a question, and won't be of much interest to you as it's visual media, but if any readers can watch BBC iplayer, there is a recent series by Adam Curtis called "can't get you out of my head" which may be of interest.

It touches on a lot of things you've written about, such as peak oil, climate change, individualism and mass consumerism.

Viewed with an occult lens, even more details stand out in the series of seemingly unconnected events.

The thing which really stood out to me is how many coincidences/synchronicities were involved in the building of the 'modern' world since the end of the second world war, and I was struck by the concept that this appeared to be a guided process, but in a way which no human could guide it. In my own synchronicity, I'd been reading a series of articles called "Losing Earth - the decade we could have saved the world" (later made into a book) just beforehand, from a friend's recommendation, at more or less the same time as you'd re-posted your articles on the same topic on the discussion here!

Thinking this over a lot, and with a mild sense of detachment since I first read your articles and the anger and sadness at the road not taken due to decisions made before I was born now faded, it feels like we were *supposed* to burn through all the fossil fuels. Whether it's to bring all the carbon up into the atmosphere instead of it being locked up and useless as coal, or to heat up the atmosphere to benefit some other species, or just to knock us off our high horse is really only for the gods themselves to know, but it does also seem to fit with our impending 4000 year long age of Saturn. After all, an irradiated, polluted, barren landscape with a hostile climate, and lives steeped in hardship, deprivation and very much rooted in the material 'here and now' plane would seem firmly in line with the Saturnian era.

It may also be that, much like bacteria on a petri dish full of agar, we would always have burned through all our fuel regardless, and the temptation to do so instead of rationing it slowly over centuries would always have been too great, especially for the benefit of generations in the far-flung future, at the cost of our own short-term comfort (though that seems far less appealing when reincarnation posits that you will return as one of those far-flung generations!)

I also believe there was something deep and dark which lived in the oil for untold aeons, and, like Pandora's box has now been released. I also believe it found a new home in the very things it now powers, which is to say, computers. And they have now got into our brains and driven us mad. Where they will go when the energy to power the computers is another matter, but in the short-to-medium term, I imagine that it will be imperative to keep the computers powered *at all costs*.

Apologies, that was more than I intended to write!

comments.title: Military “Luck” and astrology
comments.content:
Some time ago, I encountered an article analyzing the way France executed its intervention in Mali. One detail that stood out was that the officers that were sent for this operation were selected based on how ‘lucky’ they were, that is to say that they completed their missions with austere resources, taking great risks, yet coming out on top with few casualties.

One of the articles in question: https://www.rand.org/content/dam/rand/pubs/research_reports/RR700/RR770/RAND_RR770.pdf

Given the discussions on astrological timing and gaming the election of time and place to take action for maximum effect, it seems prudent that a given officer candidate could be evaluated using the essential dignities in his natal chart, as well as translocation charts for likely areas of operation (even better, a projected map that can locate bands and regions with excellent accidental placements of the houses). So my questions are the following:

1) Does this method of evaluation make sense?
2) What kind of planetary placements would a ‘lucky’ officer have? Am I on the right track assuming Mars (action) in a favorable relation to the Sun, as well as Saturn (economy of resources)?
3) What is the name of the method for projecting bands of house cups on to a map called? I recall a mundane chart that discussed the breadth of certain houses in relation to an eclipse across a geographical region, and I find that such a visual representation would be of use for making a snap chart of a given locality, particularly for mundane charts far in the future, when geopolitical borders and capital cities are changed and shifted.

replies.title: Re: Military “Luck” and astrology
replies.content:
Not the OP but I know something about this episode.
The French units that took part in the operation were very largely from two historic units (the Legion and the Troupes de Marine), which were always fully professional even during the period of national service, and always used for such overseas operations. They have very long traditions, and if you join such units, you stay with them for your whole career. They are both elite units and very difficult to join in the first place. Unlike the US system, which swaps people around like a multinational company, the French Army, and especially these units, are a "family" where everybody knows everyone, and there is an entire language of tradition and custom. In addition, the officers were traditionally recruited from aristocratic and upper-middle-class Catholic families, with a traditionalist outlook. They tended to dominate the important command positions in the Army, and to some extent still do. It's not surprising that they are militarily very effective, and that the commanders are trusted. It's a good example of something the successful military have - glamour almost - which even resembles a type of magic.

replies.title: Re: Military “Luck” and astrology
replies.content:
1) You'd be better off doing what the French did, and simply looking at the careers of the officers to choose the ones who reliably got good results.

2) You'll have to ask a natal astrologer about that. That's not my specialty, and I don't have the experience reviewing hundreds of natal charts that would be needed to answer that.

3) That's not a method I'm familiar with. The method of locality astrology I use consists of casting a chart for the moment of birth, but with the location you have in mind instead of the actual place of birth. That puts the planets into different houses, and can predict very effectively such things as economic prosperity. (My career blossomed as soon as I moved off the west coast, where several planets are in bad house placements for prosperity, to the east coast, where the houses governing prosperity are ruled by benefics. Yes, that was quite deliberate.)

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
JMG,

1. What are your thoughts on Wikipedia’s Timeline of the Far Future? To me it seems like a lot of speculation based on currently fashionable theories of the cosmos that are based on shaky experimental evidence at best. To me, it seems like the height of hubris to claim with any certainty what will happen quintillions of years from now. The timeline fulfills the same psychological needs as the old religious eschatologies but allows supposedly enlightened people to adopt the poise of being “scientific.” https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Timeline_of_the_far_future

2. I’m getting into my second year of studying magic through your books on the GD tradition and it seems like one of the underlying lessons of the Hermetic Qabalah is a certain detachment from intellectual models of the cosmos. The Qabalah uses various overlapping and sometimes incompatible systems of symbolism (the elements, the planets, the Spheres, the Tarot, etc.), and part of the learning experience is to try to make sense of it all. The student is encouraged to chase his own tail for a while in the attempt to put the pieces together (and I’ve certainly done lots of that). However, the deeper intent seems to be that of teaching the student to apply each model in its proper context and not take it as being equivalent to the Absolute itself, which remains undefinable. Symbolism, and by extension intellectual models of the cosmos invented by human beings, are treated as the finger pointing at the Moon and not the Moon itself. Perhaps the human mind is ultimately incapable of grasping the Absolute, at least in an intellectual sense. This would put the Qabalistic tradition at odds with modern claims that a “Theory of Everything” is achievable, and the Pinkerian glorification of human reason as the end-all, be-all of the cosmos. Would you agree?

replies.title: (no subject)

2) Excellent! You're paying attention. One of the basic principles of all occult philosophy is the honest and necessary recognition that human beings aren't bright enough to understand the truth about the cosmos. (Your brain is six inches long; the cosmos is billions of light years across. The difference in scale matters.) All we can do is create symbolic models and metaphors that work in certain specific fields more often than not. Rationalists like Pinker believe that the universe must be simple-mided enough that our brains can comprehend it; to me, and to the whole tradition of occult philosophy, that notion is absurdly hubristic.

comments.title: Romans
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I remember you mentioned a while ago about a practice which the Romans called something like "Latia" whereby they would effectively bribe the gods of a hostile nation to lose favour with them in exchange for temples and offerings.

While reading the Bible the other day, I was wondering if this was why the Romans had quite so many problems with the Hebrews, and later on, the Christians - their gods could not be bribed. If we assume that YHWH had decided that the Hebrews would be his chosen people, and his covenant was kept, then nothing the Romans could do would remove his favour. The same goes for Jesus - if favour is freely available to all who believe, then it cannot be taken away from anyone else who believes.

I also noticed the phrase at the end of Matthew from Jesus: "I will be with you until the end of this age". This could be me reading too much into it, but to me this implies that at the end of the Piscean age, Jesus' power and influence will fade?

I also got onto the topic of thinking that one of the biggest problems with monotheism is that you are asking a lot of your god - they must be responsible for everything from good crop harvests to love and marriage and a happy home, to victory in warfare. In a polytheistic religion, there is very much a division of labour amongst the gods, with each having a relatively specialised function. Even though the gods are the gods, and their power far exceeds ours, to put it simply - we don't ask one tool to do every task. Therefore, your chosen god MUST be all-knowing and all-powerful, and from that there comes the instinctive dogmatic assertion that it must be the only god and all things must be it's divine will alone (as opposed to competing gods with their own agendas), as otherwise it's tempting to feel like you've bet on the wrong horse... (and, taking my tool metaphor one step further - the expression "when all you have is a hammer, the whole world is a nail" springs to mind!)

Just some musings I had, anyway!

comments.title: Regarding Max Theon
comments.content: Is Max Theon's occult practice related to yours? If yes, could you do a future post on the main blog on Max Theon?

replies.title: Re: Regarding Max Theon
replies.content:
Max Theon was also claimed as the behind-the-scenes master of the adepts in the H. B. of L. (Hermetic Brotherhood of Luxor/Light), especially of Peter Davidson and T. H. Dalton or d'Alton ( = T. H. Burgoyne = Zanoni = Norman Astley). A good selection of the H. B. of L.'s teaching papers were published in 1995 by Joscelyn Godwin, Christian Chanel and John Patrick Deveney, The Hermetic Brotherhood of Luxor: Initiatic and Historical Documents of an Order of Practical Occultism. Prior to that there was an Italian publication of similar documents: H. B. of L.: Textes et documents secrets de la Hermetic Brotherhood of Luxor (Milan, 1988). A lot of their practices can be traced back to Paschal Beverly Randolph.

As "Zanoni," Burgoyne published The Light of Egypt, which was later expanded and reissued in two volumes by his followers Henry and Belle Wagner. They, in turn, handed on the torch to the man who, as "Elbert Benjamine" and "C. C. Zain," established the Church of Light, which is still active today.

Mirra Alfassa was Max Theon's long-time collaborator in Algiers. After Theon's death she went to India and met Sri Aurobindo, on whose later spiritual development she had considerable influence.

replies.title: Re: Regarding Max Theon
replies.content: I have one of those six-degrees-of-separation connections to Max Theon. Theon taught the founders of the Hermetic Brotherhood of Luxor (HB of L), which we'll be discussing soon in a post; Sylvester Gould was a member of the HB of L, and went on to found the Societas Rosicruciana in America (SRIA); I'm a member of the SRIA, among other things. I'll see if I can find enough information about Maximilian Bimstein aka Max Theon to do a post on him one of these days.

The area between 1 and 3 is the area usually reserved for compromise and a middle ground, but instead means getting hit from both ends. It had always caused me great despair in the past because it seemed there was no way out, and if you stick to the single dimension, there isn't. Later that afternoon I realized there is, so, a way out - just don't give a rat's rear end for what other people think. Or why the despair felt oddly remote this time, defused by - I thought then - old age, when you're out of that rat race.

Since reading Seneca's advice - medicine for Nero, probably, but poison for me - I'd been thinking that a circle of Greek goddesses around the mirror on my altar would serve to deflect Inner Watcher, and put them up late yesterday afternoon in a staggered pyramid: Minerva on top, Diana and Hecate down the right side, Aphrodite and Bast on the left between the mirror and my family photos above the head of the bed; like my altar, on the northwest wall I've designated as North. And just before bedtime meditation, realized Aesop had said it all, ages ago, in the parable of the old man, the boy, the donkey, and four different sets of passers-by. I sat down to meditate with only the light of one candle as usual, and could feel the power. And the music in my mind's ear - a side effect of tinnitus and hearing loss - kicked in with a bass chorus of Beethoven's Ode To Joy. And I went to bed on that note.

- and could not sleep. The chorus went on and on, and I felt keyed up, and was already running on short sleep from the night before. I could control it a bit, to get The Star-Spangled Banner, The Battle Hymn of the Republic, and other songs of triumph. None of the rebel songs that so sustained me back in the evil days. It took an enormous effort to ground out enough to be able to sleep at all! And included pouring a libation of red wine to the goddesses, with a crisp gingersnap. And reading (The Goat Foot God - realized I'd probably reached the Goddesses through Pan and them, just as High reached his hods through Pan and Mona. It was two hours before I came back to normal. And just like carrying a current too strong for my circuits; it had to be stepped down to my carrying capacity.

What happened here? Whatever it was, I know it was for the good. The mantra about "not giving a rat's rear end" is, always and forever, modified by "For the good of all and with harm to none,and as long as I retain my good manners." "..An it harm none, do as ye will."

One more trigger - I'd had a deep and frank discussion with my daughter about trusting her - first, with my finances (95% confident; she settled that by reminding me that her dad, deep in dementia, has 4 times my assets, and if she'd wanted to rob anybody, he'd be the target. But she does have her dad's financial integrity, and is not in need.); then about my fears of being judged insane because of the unusual (well, not in s/f fandom, or among your readers) way my mind works, and she reminded me that if anybody understands where I'm coming from, it's her; she's made it her life's work. (Autism, OCD, phobias ...) and that night my defensiveness around her, fear of being judged, fell clean away in the face of that simple fact.

Oddly enogh, yesterday;s card draw included Lightning Bolt for Present, and today's draw, the same, for Past. with Hearthfire for Present and Shepherd for Future. You can't get more confirmation than that. So - what happened?

The Grey Badger, a.k.a Patricia Mathews

Best of luck.

OBOD seems like a fine organization, but there's something about the candidacy curriculum of AODA that seems like what I might need for my development to start.

I have some resistance. Due to my fundy Christian (Jehovah's Witness in my case) upbringing and natural individualist streak I am not much of a joiner. I recently finished Mark Stavish's fine book on Egregores and feel more justified than ever that I have some good reasons for this.

I did a Tarot spread a few weeks ago about this and the cards that seemed most potent were the Tower (i interpreted this to mean, I should expect to have my foundations rocked if I undertake this) The Ace of Swords (I am being offered a new perspective through which to see things) and the 10 of Cups (There is a chance to experience a sense of community and connection I haven't allowed myself in some time.)

That's a lot of background, but it seemed important to elaborate at least a little

Questions.

1.) If I switch from LBRP to learning the SOP, is there anything I should be alert to in the lag time between gaining proficiency? daily practice of the LBRP has had a big effect both on my physical space and the way I show up in the world, and I would like to keep that momentum in tact to the extent that I can.

2.) Is there anything I should specifically do to help reconcile my explorations of Celtic myth and imagery with my Christian background and disposition?

I have been praying on the topic for awhile and have received no obvious red lights in response, but I would like to ask you and the commentariat for any insights that might be worthwhile.

Thank you again for this forum and for any advice you may have.

-SOF

comments.title: Words and Intent
comments.content:
Archdruid,

When I cast spells I build my intention by meditating very carefully on the words I use. Part of this exercise means looking through an etymology dictionary to find what a word originally meant. You pointed out in one of your previous responses to me that words are initially significantly more accurate when they’re first used, and gradually lose their power as they become popular. I've found the practice of finding the original meaning of words tremendously helpful because it finally helped me understand the difference between conscious intent and unconscious intent.

The latest example of this was "entrepreneur.” I remember the word gradually replacing "businessperson" in the mid-90s, when business became a dirty word on college campuses. I recently looked up the original meaning of the word and I burst out laughing at what I found. The word initially meant "the manager or promoter of a theatrical production," and went on to mean "to manage."

Does this explain how farcically our economy has become? Our society collective chants a word to describe a person who starts a business enterprise, but gets an endless legion of managers who stage elaborate rituals to make a buck, adding no real value to our national prosperity.

Regards,

Varun

replies.title: Re: Words and Intent
replies.content: Hmm! That makes a great deal of sense.

Hi JMG,

So, I've seen some mention recently by some that divination using Tarot cards (rather than just playing games with them) was originated by the Romani, and many members of the Romani are strongly opposed to folk outside the community using them for such. My question for you is twofold:

1) Is there any historical evidence for this claim? I cannot for the life of me find any credible evidence for it anywhere...

2) What are your thoughts more generally about when it is or is not acceptable to adopt a magical or religious practice that originated with an ethnic group to which you do not belong?

Thanks again,
Ryan M.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) The tarot was invented in Renaissance Italy around 1415; it was first used for divination by French occultists around 1800. The Rom borrowed it, and so have no claim to any exclusive right to it.

2) If it originated within that ethnic group, remains a living tradition within that ethnic group, exists nowhere outside that ethnic group, and you aren't invited to take part by the qualified members of that ethnic group, it's polite to leave it strictly alone. If those criteria aren't met, I wouldn't worry about it.

comments.title: Dolmen Arch, Volume 1
comments.content:
Hello -

I am working my way through the first Dolmen Arch volume, and have come across a tricky area on the negative/positive states/attitudes of the seven cantrefs (as found on page 191). For the third and fourth cantrefs, have the negative states gotten reversed? Here is what is in the book:

Ufel: negative state, anger / positive attitude, courage
Daear: negative state, fear / positive attitude, wisdom

However, the example that immediately follows it envisions countering anger with wisdom, which to me suggests that the negative states of Ufel/Daear should be reversed from how they are printed in the book to read:

Ufel: negative state, fear / positive attitude, courage
Daear: negative state, anger / positive attitude, wisdom

A typo, or is there a subtle point that I am completely missing?

Thank you, and greetings from your former stomping grounds (Cumberland).

replies.title: Re: Dolmen Arch, Volume 1
replies.content:
It's not a typo. The negative states are not the opposites of the positive states, they're distorted and weak versions of the positive states. For Ufel, which is the fire cantref, anger is the weak negative state while courage is the strong positive expression; both are ways of confronting an enemy, for example. For Daear, which is the earth cantref, fear is the weak negative state while wisdom is the strong positive expression; both are ways of responding to the kind of dangers you can't fight, for example.

As for the example that follows, note that Ufel and Daear are opposite one another, south and north respectively; meditate on that, and see what you find!

replies.title: Re: Dolmen Arch, Volume 1
replies.content: Oh my, that is much more subtle and interesting. Thank you!

comments.title: Astral, etheric, or other effects as a result of unrequited love?
comments.content:
I am curious if there is an astral, etheric, or other effect above the material plane that arises from Person A having a crush on/infatuation with/obsession with/unrequited love for Person B that is not returned.

Assuming the answer is yes,
Would there still be any effects if Person B was an actress who has been deceased for decades? (for example, Marilyn Monroe)

Iirc, you somewhat addressed this a while ago, but I can't find the relevant comments. All I recall was something to the effect of:
-Person B is often repulsed by Person A as a result.
-This is why celebrities are often on drugs, because they're compensating for a severe astral drain from so many admirers.

replies.title: Re: Astral, etheric, or other effects as a result of unrequited love?
replies.content:
This is all part of polarity theory, which is discussed in symbolic form in Dion Fortune's The Cosmic Doctrine and in much less veiled form in their novels. Love is a force; when you get someone pouring out adoration toward another person, and it's not returned, the force has to be discharged in some other way; if you have a whole bunch of people doing this toward one person, and the person doesn't know how to use that in magic, they tend to get into drugs or various other kinds of excess just to discharge the force. (That's why clergy, who no longer get adequate training in handling energy, so often run off the rails these days.)

If the adoration is aimed at someone who isn't incarnate, their image becomes a charged presence on the inner planes, and anyone who knows how to draw on it can put it to work in various magical ways, some wholesome, some the opposite. Alternatively, if nobody does so, you're probably going to get an egregor powerful enough to act like a deity, at least for a while.
